ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for a customisable grain bowl with egg and three toppings. FOODON_00002473 (grain bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — grain-based mixed dish with egg and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for a customisable grain bowl with egg and three toppings. A0CKP (composite dish, grain-based) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Toppings and sauce-free preparation are noted as modifiers.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a customisable grain bowl food product. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this generic mixed dish record.

main_ingredients: cooked grains (e.g. brown rice, quinoa, farro, or mixed grains)
three variable toppings (e.g. vegetables, legumes, tofu, or lean protein)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical grain bowl portion as served at Singaporean health-food stalls and cafes, comprising a grain base, one egg, and three toppings with no sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Grain bowl with egg (3 toppings), no sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Grain bowl with egg (3 toppings), no sauce' (cr_id F08010033). All per-100g macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron values are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95; saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central egg and grain composition data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain bowl analogous estimate based on cooked brown rice (GI 50) and mixed vegetable/egg composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable grain bowl. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked brown rice (GI ~50, University of Sydney) as the primary grain base analogue, adjusted downward to 48 to account for the diluting effect of egg protein (GI negligible), dietary fibre (1.6 g/100g), and mixed vegetable toppings which lower the overall glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (48) × available carbohydrate per serving (11.08 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 = 48 × 38.78 / 100 ≈ 18.6 / 100 × 100 = GL ≈ 5.9 per 100g equivalent portion basis. GL per serving (350g) = 48 × (11.08 × 3.5) / 100 = 48 × 38.78 / 100 ≈ 18.6; reported as 5.9 per 100g-equivalent for consistency with per-100g nutrient reporting. Estimation is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 137 mg/100g — well below the 400 mg/100g threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: This sauce-free grain bowl is suitable for hypertensive patients; sodium at 137 mg/100g is low, and patients should ensure any protein toppings are not pre-marinated in high-sodium sauces to maintain the overall low-sodium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~48) and moderate carbohydrate content (11.08 g/100g) with egg protein supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~48) and moderate carbohydrate load of this grain bowl make it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management; selecting a whole-grain base (brown rice, quinoa) and including legume toppings will further reduce the glycaemic response and improve satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.82 g/100g), low sodium (137 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (1.6 g/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This grain bowl is suitable for cardiovascular disease patients due to its low saturated fat, low sodium, and fibre content; patients should choose unsaturated-fat-rich toppings such as avocado or edamame and avoid adding high-sodium sauces to preserve the favourable lipid and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 137 mg/100g well below 400 mg RA threshold; egg provides vitamin D; vegetable toppings contribute antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This bowl is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; patients should specify toppings to avoid nightshade vegetables if symptoms are active and prioritise cruciferous vegetables such as broccoli or bok choy to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A grain bowl with egg and three toppings (no sauce) is a customisable, health-focused mixed dish popular in Singapore's contemporary café and health-food stall scene. The base consists of cooked whole grains — typically brown rice, quinoa, farro, or a mixed-grain blend — providing complex carbohydrates and dietary fibre. One egg (boiled, poached, or pan-fried) is added as the primary protein source, contributing approximately 4.4 g protein per 100 g of the assembled bowl. Three variable toppings are selected from options such as steamed or roasted vegetables, legumes, tofu, edamame, or lean protein pieces. The sauce is intentionally omitted, significantly reducing sodium and added sugar compared to sauced variants. The resulting dish is nutritionally balanced: 92 kcal/100g, 4.41 g protein, 3.31 g fat, 11.08 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 137 mg sodium per 100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 48 (low GI), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. The dish is suitable for patients managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-friendly. The flavour profile is mild and savoury, with texture varying by topping selection. This record represents the generic, sauce-free variant as catalogued in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010033).
common_user_queries: Is grain bowl with egg healthy?
Calories in grain bowl with egg no sauce

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010033) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central egg and grain composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked brown rice as the primary analogue. All other micronutrients are set to -1 as they are not reported in the HPB record for this composite dish.
notes: Saturated fat (0.82 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked egg (USDA FDC ID 748967, hard-boiled egg: ~1.0 g sat fat/100g) and cooked brown rice (~0.2 g sat fat/100g), weighted approximately 25% egg and 75% grain-and-vegetable base, yielding ~0.82 g/100g. This is an approximation as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific assembled product as of 2026. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on cooked brown rice (University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted for egg protein and vegetable toppings; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycaemic load per serving (350g) = approximately 18.6 g GL units; reported as 5.9 in the per-100g-equivalent field for consistency. Dietary flags: halal status assumes egg and toppings are from halal-certified sources as is standard in Singapore HPB-catalogued dishes; gluten-free status assumes grain base does not include wheat-based grains. Vegetarian flag is set true as the dish contains egg but no meat; vegan is false due to egg content.
